definition: Deep learning is a branch of machine learning that trains multi-layer neural networks to learn hierarchical representations of data end-to-end from examples, rather than relying primarily on hand-crafted features.
layman: Deep learning is machine learning where stacked layers of simple computing units learn useful patterns directly from raw data — like pixels, text, or audio — without you manually engineering every feature.
content:
## What deep learning is

**Deep learning (DL)** is a **subset of machine learning** that uses **neural networks with multiple layers** to **learn representations** — internal features — directly from data.

This topic is the **canonical home** for the DL definition in this roadmap: **multi-layer neural networks** plus **representation learning**.

### The two defining ideas

1. **Neural networks** — composable layers of neurons with weights, biases, and nonlinear activations that transform inputs into outputs.
2. **Representation learning** — the network discovers useful intermediate features (edges → shapes → objects; characters → words → meaning) through training, instead of an engineer specifying those features upfront.

### How DL fits the AI / ML / DL / Gen AI stack

| Layer | Role | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **AI** | Broad goal: systems that perform tasks requiring intelligence | Chess engines, chatbots, recommenders |
| **Machine learning** | Learn patterns from data instead of only hard-coded rules | Spam filters, demand forecasting |
| **Deep learning** | ML with multi-layer neural nets and learned representations | Image classifiers, speech recognition |
| **Generative AI** | Models that create new content (often built on DL) | Text, image, and code generators |

DL is **not** all of AI or all of ML. Classical ML (trees, SVMs, linear models on engineered features) remains widely used. DL excels when data are high-dimensional, unstructured, and abundant.

### Why "deep"?

**Depth** means stacking many layers so each layer builds on the previous one.

- A single hidden layer can approximate many functions, but **depth** often learns **hierarchical structure** more efficiently.
- Early layers may capture simple patterns; deeper layers combine them into complex concepts.

There is no universal layer count that makes a model "deep," but in practice DL means **more than a shallow stack** — typically several to hundreds of layers depending on architecture and era.

### Representation learning in practice

In classical ML you might hand-craft features: histogram of oriented gradients for images, bag-of-words for text.

In DL:

- **Convolutional layers** learn spatial filters for vision.
- **Recurrent or attention layers** learn sequential structure for language and time series.
- **Embeddings** map discrete tokens into continuous vectors that capture similarity.

Training adjusts all layer weights jointly via **gradient-based optimization** (details in Multilayer Perceptron and framework topics).

### What DL is good at

- **Unstructured data**: images, audio, video, long text.
- **Large datasets**: enough examples for millions or billions of parameters.
- **End-to-end pipelines**: raw input → model → task output with one differentiable objective.

### What this topic does not cover (see linked topics)

- **Perceptron / MLP mechanics** — layers, activations, backpropagation → Perceptron & Multilayer Perceptron.
- **Framework APIs and training loops** → Deep Learning Frameworks.
- **Generative modeling goals and products** → Generative AI.

Understanding DL at this level means knowing **where it sits**, **what makes it distinct** (depth + learned representations), and **when it is the right tool** — before diving into architectures and code.
keyPoints: Deep learning is ML with multi-layer neural networks that learn representations end-to-end from data.
This topic is the canonical definition home for DL, neural nets, and representation learning in the roadmap.
DL specializes in unstructured, high-dimensional data where hand-crafted features are costly or weak.
Depth enables hierarchical features: simple patterns in early layers, complex concepts in deeper layers.
Modern generative AI is largely built on deep learning, but DL is broader than generation alone.
